data.title: AI Won’t ‘Kill’ Bitcoin Mining – It Might Reprice It
data.description:
Since the latest Bitcoin halving cut block rewards in half, compressing revenues across the mining sector, large operators have been searching for ways to stabilize income streams.

Increasingly, they are leasing their energy footprints to artificial intelligence and high-performance computing clients. The model is not speculative; it is already being written into multiyear contracts.

In September, Cipher Mining signed a 168-megawatt agreement with Fluidstack, an AI cloud provider. The deal runs for ten years and is valued at $3 billion. Google provided financing support worth $1.4 billion and also acquired a 5% equity stake in Cipher.

The arrangement allows Cipher to maintain ownership of its facilities while converting part of its power allocation into contracted AI revenue.

Cipher Mining Signs 168 MW, 10-Year AI Hosting Agreement with FluidstackIn this transaction, Cipher will deliver 168 MW of Critical IT Load to Fluidstack at its Barber Lake site, securing ~$3B in contracted revenue over the initial 10-year term. The agreement also includes two…— Cipher Mining (@CipherInc)

TeraWulf, another U.S.-based miner, followed a similar path. It announced hosting agreements that dedicate more than 200 megawatts to AI workloads at its Lake Mariner site. Analysts estimate the value of the deal could exceed $3.7 billion.Contracts That Change Miner Balance Sheets

The financial character of these companies is beginning to change. Miner equity has historically traded with a high correlation to the price of Bitcoin. New long-dated contracts give investors a different risk profile to consider. Regular dollar-denominated payments from AI customers may reduce the exposure of miner stocks to Bitcoin cycles.

Iren, an Australian operator, provides an example. It recently expanded its by purchasing more than 12,000 GPUs. The company projects $500 million in annual AI revenue by early 2026. Analysts at Arete initiated coverage on Iren, Riot Platforms, and Cipher Mining with buy ratings, citing the stability of contracted AI revenue as a driver.

The case of CoreWeave and Core Scientific stresses the point. CoreWeave, once an Ethereum miner, shifted into GPU-based hosting. In 2025, it acquired Core Scientific in a transaction valued at $9 billion. The deal cemented its place as a supplier of computing power for AI firms, moving entirely beyond token mining.Why the AI Shift Is Different

The entry into AI hosting is not simply diversification. It forces miners to rethink operations. Unlike Bitcoin mining, AI customers demand strict service level agreements. Data centers must offer redundancy, cooling efficiency, and long-term maintenance commitments. In practice, this means capital is redeployed from short-cycle ASIC purchases toward infrastructure upgrades that support higher-density workloads.

There is also the allocation question. Every megawatt committed to AI hosting cannot be used for Bitcoin mining. Operators will have to balance the immediate predictability of contracted revenue with the option value of a potential Bitcoin price rally.From Hashprice to Lease Price

The mining business has long been tied to hashprice, the dollar value of one terahash of computing power per day. That metric is now being supplemented by what could be called lease price, the value of contracted power sold to external clients. In time, the lease price may become as influential for valuation models as the hash price itself.

This shift has implications for the broader network. If miners dedicate more capacity to external hosting, the growth of network hash rate may slow.

That could alter the competitive dynamics among remaining pure miners and impact difficulty adjustments. At the same time, the capital stability provided by AI contracts could keep some firms alive through periods of low Bitcoin prices, preventing sharp declines in total hash rate.A Changing Capital Formation Cycle

The sector’s capital cycle is also adjusting. Past expansions were often financed during bull markets when high margins justified the rapid purchase of machines. Now, multi-year AI contracts provide the collateral base for raising capital in less favorable markets. That changes the rhythm of how mining infrastructure is built.

The long-term outcome is not that AI erases mining. Rather, it layers another economic activity on top of the same infrastructure. For investors, miner equities may look less like high-beta proxies for Bitcoin and more like hybrid firms that combine commodity-linked income with contracted service revenue.

data.title: Alight, Iridium, CDW, First Advantage, and Xerox Shares Are Soaring, What You Need To Know
data.description:
What Happened?

A number of stocks jumped in the afternoon session after the latest Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Price Index report aligned with economists' predictions, signaling that inflation is under control. 

The Core PCE Price Index, which is closely watched by the Federal Reserve as it excludes volatile food and energy costs, rose 0.2% month-over-month and 2.9% year-over-year. These figures precisely matched Wall Street estimates. This alignment provides relief for investors and reinforces the market's belief that inflationary pressures are being managed without harming economic growth. The positive market reaction highlights a growing conviction that the Federal Reserve will likely proceed with its anticipated path of interest rate cuts, which would make capital cheaper and help stimulate further economic expansion.

The stock market overreacts to news, and big price drops can present good opportunities to buy high-quality stocks.

Among others, the following stocks were impacted:

  • Professional Staffing & HR Solutions company Alight jumped 3.4%. Is now the time to buy Alight? Access our full analysis report here, it’s free.
  • Satellite Telecommunication Services company Iridium jumped 4.6%. Is now the time to buy Iridium? Access our full analysis report here, it’s free.
  • IT Distribution & Solutions company CDW jumped 4.2%. Is now the time to buy CDW? Access our full analysis report here, it’s free.
  • Professional Staffing & HR Solutions company First Advantage jumped 3%. Is now the time to buy First Advantage? Access our full analysis report here, it’s free.
  • Hardware & Infrastructure company Xerox jumped 3.3%. Is now the time to buy Xerox? Access our full analysis report here, it’s free.

Zooming In On Iridium (IRDM)

Iridium’s shares are quite volatile and have had 18 moves greater than 5% over the last year. In that context, today’s move indicates the market considers this news meaningful but not something that would fundamentally change its perception of the business.

The previous big move we wrote about was 14 days ago when the stock dropped 3.3% on the news that Raymond James downgraded the stock to "Outperform" from "Strong Buy" and significantly lowered its price target. 

The price target was adjusted to $26, down from the previous $39. The downgrade follows a strategic move by rival SpaceX Starlink, which announced the purchase of spectrum rights to intensify its push into the Direct-to-Device market. This acquisition has heightened competitive concerns within the satellite communications sector, pressuring Iridium's stock, which had already fallen approximately 29% this week. The analyst action reflects growing uncertainty about Iridium's position in an increasingly competitive landscape.

Iridium is down 40.1% since the beginning of the year, and at $17.68 per share, it is trading 47.3% below its 52-week high of $33.57 from October 2024. Investors who bought $1,000 worth of Iridium’s shares 5 years ago would now be looking at an investment worth $702.10.

data.title: Oil Posts Weekly Gains on Russia-Ukraine Tensions — Market Talk
data.description: Crude futures close the week higher, spurred by continuing Ukrainian strikes against Russian oil infrastructure and the prospect of additional EU and U.S. sanctions that raise concerns of supply constraint. WTI settles up 1.1% at $65.72 a barrel, a 5.3% weekly gain and highest close since early August. Executives polled Sept. 10-18 by the Dallas Fed lowered their average year-end WTI price expectation to $63.06 a barrel from $68.18 in the 2Q survey. Responses ranged from $50 to $80 a barrel, with 43% seeing the year-end price between $60 and $64.99, and 33% between $65 and $69.99. Brent settles up 1% at $70.13 a barrel, up 5.2% on the week. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1517 ET - Crude futures close the week higher, spurred by continuing Ukrainian strikes against Russian oil infrastructure and the prospect of additional EU and U.S. sanctions that raise concerns of supply constraint. WTI settles up 1.1% at $65.72 a barrel, a 5.3% weekly gain and highest close since early August. Executives polled Sept. 10-18 by the Dallas Fed lowered their average year-end WTI price expectation to $63.06 a barrel from $68.18 in the 2Q survey. Responses ranged from $50 to $80 a barrel, with 43% seeing the year-end price between $60 and $64.99, and 33% between $65 and $69.99. Brent settles up 1% at $70.13 a barrel, up 5.2% on the week. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1354 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. increased by six to 424 in a fifth straight weekly rise, and was 60 below the year-ago level, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. In the Dallas Fed's 3Q survey of energy companies, 42% of exploration and production executives said they have slightly delayed investment decisions because of oil price uncertainty or cost of production, and 36% said they have significantly delayed decisions. On regulatory changes made since January, 57% said they had reduced break-even costs for new wells by less than $1 a barrel, and 25% estimated reductions between $1 and $1.99 a barrel. Rigs directed at natural gas this week slipped by one to 117, or 18 more than a year ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1018 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are lower heading into the expiration of the October contract following gains the previous three session. The market is seeing little support from the weather with a mild start to October likely to delay early-season heating demand. "Even as LNG begins to rise seasonally, the near-term fundamental outlook for natural gas is bearish as storage fills rapidly and weather remains mild," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. The October contract is off 2.8% at $2.823/mmBtu. Gas for November delivery is down 1.8% at $3.138/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1011 ET - Oil futures are higher on rising geopolitical risk premium with continuing Ukraine attacks on Russian oil facilities,and President Trump's rekindling of tariff battles with duties on pharmaceuticals and heavy trucks. Although most of the buying has been by short-sided traders liquidating their positions, "the crude market has gained merit on steady demand seemingly keeping up with added supplies from OPEC+," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. The latest tariffs may signal to India and China "that additional tariffs could be heading their way if they remain buyers of Russian crude," he adds.WTI is up 0.8% at $65.48 a barrel and Brent gains 0.6% to $69.83. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0911 ET - Canada GDP is on track on for 0.8% annualized growth in 3Q following the release of July GDP data, says Andrew Grantham, economist at CIBC Capital Markets. GDP by industry climbed 0.2% in July from the prior month, powered by Canada's oil-and-gas sector, marking the first month-over-month increase since March. But the Statistics Canada says early indicators suggest GDP was flat in August, which "suggests that the economy stalled again that month," Grantham says. The Bank of Canada forecast a 1% annualized gain for 3Q. Grantham says the BOC will likely lean on the pending employment and inflation data before deciding on whether to cut rates again on October 29. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0517 ET - Crude oil exports from Western Russia surged to a 10-month high this month driven by a sharp increase in Urals crude loadings amid intensifying Ukrainian drone strikes against key infrastructure, Kpler says. "Following these attacks, Russian crude oil runs are expected to slip to an annual low of below 5 million barrels a day in September," says senior market analyst Yui Torikata. "This forces producers to redirect crude that would have been processed domestically to the export market, with Kpler expecting seaborne crude oil exports to serve as a primary outlet for this displaced crude in 4Q." Exports have averaged around 2.5 million barrels a day in September, a level not reached since October 2024, and are projected to stay at about 2.25 million barrels a day if all scheduled loadings are completed by the end of the month, according to the data provider. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0405 ET - Oil prices are on track for a significant weekly gain following news that Russia plans to temporarily ban diesel exports from resellers and extend its gasoline-export ban amid Ukrainian drone strikes against refineries. In early trade, Brent crude ticks 0.1% higher to $68.66 a barrel, while WTI is up 0.2% to $65.13 a barrel. The benchmarks continue to trade in a narrow range, but are up 3% and 4.4% on the week, respectively. Geopolitical tensions are high, with European diplomats warning the Kremlin that NATO is prepared to take action against further violations of its airspace, including by shooting down Russian planes, according to a Bloomberg report. However, further gains are capped by uncertainties over the macroeconomic outlook, with President Trump saying he would slap new tariffs on pharma and large trucks, and a looming global supply surplus.(giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0332 ET - China's new 2035 target of 3,600GW of solar and wind power capacity looks conservative and will likely be exceeded, Deutsche Bank analyst Gary Zhou says. He expects supply-side reform to remain the near-term focus, as restoring supply-demand balance in the renewable sector is key for investors. On the demand side, Zhou forecasts China will add 1,455GW of solar and 600GW of wind power capacities during the 2026-2030 period, well above additions in 2021-2025. DB's top renewables pick is GCL Tech, citing its low valuation and potential gains from solar supply reforms. Shares last traded at HK$1.27. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0252 ET - The Philippine port sector's outlook appears positive, Maybank Securities' Germaine Guinto says in a research report. The ports' container volumes gained strong momentum in 1H and are poised to quicken in 2H, the analyst says. Growth is anchored by resilient domestic consumption and a pickup in trade activity, with foreign containers accounting for the majority of the ports' traffic, the analyst says. International Container Terminal Services, via Manila International Container Terminal port's dominant gateway role, remains best-placed to capture this momentum, the analyst adds. The brokerage has a buy rating and a target price of PHP590.00 on International Container Terminal Services shares, which are 0.6% lower at PHP495.80. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.description:
Following a public comment period, the Federal Trade Commission has approved a final order to settle Federal Trade Commission charges that Omnicom Group Inc.'s $13.5 billion acquisition of The Interpublic Group of Companies, Inc.

data.title: Front Month ICE Brent Crude Rose 5.17% This Week to Settle at $70.13 — Data Talk
data.description:
Front Month ICE Brent Crude for Nov. delivery gained $3.45 per barrel, or 5.17% to $70.13 this week

  • Largest one week net and percentage gain since the week ending June 13, 2025
  • Up four of the past six weeks
  • Today it is up 71.00 cents or 1.02%
  • Up for four consecutive sessions
  • Up $3.56 or 5.35% over the last four sessions
  • Largest four day dollar and percentage gain since Thursday, July 31, 2025
  • Longest winning streak since Monday, Aug. 25, 2025, when the market rose for four straight sessions
  • Highest settlement value since Thursday, July 31, 2025
  • Off 14.51% from its 52-week high of $82.03 hit Wednesday, Jan. 15, 2025
  • Up 16.44% from its 52-week low of $60.23 hit Monday, May 5, 2025
  • Down 2.57% from 52 weeks ago
  • Off 14.51% from its 2025 settlement high of $82.03 hit Wednesday, Jan. 15, 2025
  • Up 16.44% from its 2025 settlement low of $60.23 hit Monday, May 5, 2025
  • Off 51.99% from its record high of $146.08 hit Thursday, July 3, 2008
  • Month-to-date it is up 2.95%
  • Year-to-date it is down $4.51 or 6.04%

All prices are calculated based on the settlement price of the current front month contract.

Source: Dow Jones Market Data

data.title: Wheat Futures Slide as Harvesting Efforts Near End — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Wheat for December delivery fell 1.5% to $5.19 1/4 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade Friday in response to the winter wheat harvest being essentially finished, and that material making it out of the fields is now being accumulated in farmers' bushel bins before being sold.
  • Corn for December delivery fell 1% to $4.21 3/4 a bushel.
  • Soybeans for November delivery rose 0.2% to $10.14 1/4 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

End of the Line: Winter wheat is essentially fully harvested in the U.S. Plains, which means that new grain is arriving at grain elevators and pressuring CBOT prices. That's why wheat futures are leading the complex lower in afternoon trading.

"Not a lot to impact the U.S. wheat crop right now as it starts to head into dormancy in a few weeks and current weather is quite favorable," said Karl Setzer of Consus Ag Consulting in a note.

Pressure Point: The expected inflow of freshly harvested crops coming from U.S. fields kept a lid on prices Friday.

"Record U.S. crop could weigh on prices through harvest," said ADM Investor Services in a note, in reference to corn.

Analysts said that corn fields will largely be the first fields worked by farmers heading into October, with soybean fields worked after.

Gaping Hole: Traders continue to speculate what China's continued absence in buying U.S. grain exports mean for overall U.S. soybean export demand.

"Soybeans remain in limbo as the market reconciles little export interest will likely remain for a few more weeks," said Rich Nelson of Allendale Inc., adding that Chinese demand could again return by December or January.

Soybeans have been under pressure throughout September, shedding roughly 4%.

INSIGHT

Macro Environment: The 25 basis-point rate cut by the Federal Reserve, along with comments suggesting that more may soon come, propelled commodity futures to a strong month, particularly in precious and industrial metals, said Ole Hansen of Saxo Bank in a note.

"The single most important tailwind for commodities this month has been the renewed easing cycle by the U.S. Federal Reserve," Hansen said.

Expectations of more rate cuts before the year is out are buoying strength in commodities.

But balancing out gains in metals is weakness in soft commodities such as cocoa, coffee and sugar, along with agricultural commodities like soybeans.

Getting to the Bottom of Things: A joint task force partnering the USDA and Justice Department to investigate the high costs for farmer inputs such as fertilizer and seeds is getting praise from groups like the National Farmers Union.

"When just a handful of companies control the markets for seed, fertilizer and other farm supplies, they can raise prices at will," the NFU said in a note. "This leaves farmers squeezed between skyrocketing costs and low prices for their products."

CBOT grain futures are mostly below the levels needed for farmers to turn a profit on their acres, and these farmers need both improved crop prices and lower input costs for these acres to turn positive.

AHEAD

  • The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly grain export inspections report at 11 a.m. EDT Monday.
  • The USDA is due to release its weekly crop progress report at 4 p.m. EDT Monday.
  • The USDA is scheduled to release its quarterly grain stocks report at noon EDT Tuesday.
  • The USDA is due to release its monthly agricultural prices report at 3 p.m. EDT Tuesday.
  • Cal-Maine Foods is scheduled to release its fiscal first-quarter 2026 earnings report at 4:05 p.m. EDT Wednesday.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: Electronic Arts Stock Pops On Report Videogame Maker Is Close to Going Private — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Angela Palumbo

Electronic Arts stock jumped on Friday after The Wall Street Journal reported that the videogame maker is close to making a deal that would take it private.

The Journal reported that a group of investors that includes private-equity firm Silver Lake and Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund are nearing a deal with EA — the videogame maker for the Madden NFL and Battlefield series — to take it private, citing people familiar with the matter.

Barron's has reached out to EA, Silver Lake, and the Saudi Arabia's Private Investment Fund for comment.

EA currently has a market capitalization of about $42 billion. The Journal reports that while discussions of a price are still ongoing, EA could be valued at around $50 billion.

Shares of EA soared 16% in afternoon trading to $195.52 and were on pace for a new high, according to Dow Jones Market Data. The stock was also the best performer in the S&P 500 on Friday.

This is breaking news. Please come back for more updates and analysis.

Write to Angela Palumbo at angela.palumbo@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 3 PM ET: Inflation rises again, PCE shows, but not enough to alarm the Fed | Stocks ...
data.description:
Inflation rises again, PCE shows, but not enough to alarm the Fed

A key measure of inflation rose at a slightly elevated pace in August, but not enough to suggest much bigger increases tied to U.S. tariffs are on the way.

----

Stocks Climb After Trump Unveils New Tariffs

U.S. stocks were higher, putting the Dow on track to break a three-day losing streak after President Trump unveiled new tariffs on drugs, trucks and furniture.

----

Consumer Sentiment Fell in September, According to University of Michigan Survey

The monthly survey's sentiment index fell to 55.1 in September, from 58.2 in August amid worries about high prices and the labor market.

----

Canada Economy Stalls in August After First Growth in Four Months

Canada's economy is on track to eke out tepid growth for the latest quarter, avoiding a second consecutive contraction.

----

Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Jobs Data in Focus

Investors will hone in on U.S. jobs data, where any signs of weakness could reignite prospects of a series of interest-rate cuts in the months to come.

----

U.S. GDP data look strong, but labor market seems weak. How key economists make sense of the mixed signals.

"The consumer is frankly exhausted," Richmond Fed President Tom Barkin said Friday.

----

Tokyo's Price Growth Steady But Rate Increase Still in Play

The pace of price growth in the Tokyo metropolitan area unexpectedly held steady in September, but upward pressure remains, keeping an interest-rate hike in play.

----

Trump to Impose New Tariffs on Pharma, Big Trucks

Levies on drugs will be imposed on companies that aren't building plants in the U.S.

----

Europe's Right-Wing Populists Swerve Left on the Economy

The adoption of a high-spending, pro-welfare and protectionist agenda has been key in winning over blue-collar workers.

----

Fed Independence Reaches Its Moment of Truth as Supreme Court Weighs Cook's Fate

The court is poised to decide whether President Trump can remove Lisa Cook from the Federal Reserve's board, which former officials see as threat to central bank independence.

----

Democrats Dig In on Shutdown Stance After White House Threatens to Fire Workers

Republicans want a short-term deal to keep the government open, while Democratic leaders seek healthcare funds.

----

Bank of Mexico Makes 10th Straight Interest-Rate Cut

The board of governors voted 4-1 to lower the overnight interest-rate target to 7.50% from 7.75%.

Sinclair Will Bring Kimmel Back to Its ABC Affiliates

Big Pharma Unfazed by Trump Tariffs, But Small Biotechs Face Vulnerability

Banks Ordered to Dig Through Account Closures to Find 'Debanking' Cases

Regulators demand information going back years as part of President Trump's crackdown on alleged political or religious discrimination.

AstraZeneca to Offer Discounted Drugs As Trump Pressures Pharma Industry to Cut Prices

AstraZeneca said it will offer its asthma and diabetes drugs at an up to 70% discount in the U.S. ahead of a Trump administration deadline for pharmaceutical companies to cut drug prices.

Amgen to Invest $650 Million in U.S. Production Expansion as Trump Hikes Tariffs

Meta to Launch Ad-Free Service on Facebook, Instagram in U.K.

The move matches an ad-free service launched in the EU in 2023, but hasn't been rolled out to the U.S. or other parts of the world.

U.S. Takes Aim at Chip Makers With New Plan to Throttle Imports

Six Flags Is Under Activist Pressure to Sell Its Real Estate

----

How Zillow Got On Track for First Profitable Year Since 2012

----

GTCR to Buy Dentalcorp in Deal Valuing Dental Network at About $1.58 Billion

----

Why Meta Thinks It Can Challenge Apple in Consumer AI Devices

Meta is betting that the future of artificial intelligence takes users way beyond smartphones and personal computers.

data.title: AI Deployment Beginning to Weigh on Job Creation, Former Central Banker Says — Market Talk
data.description: The increased use of artificial intelligence by companies and organization is likely a factor in a recent string of "pretty bad" jobs data in Canada, says Stephen Poloz, the former Bank of Canada governor. During a quarterly presentation hosted by his employer, Toronto law firm Osler Hoskin & Harcourt, Poloz says the current period of tariff-fueled elevated uncertainty has companies jumping on opportunities to cut costs. Among them is automation, or the deployment of AI. "I think we're starting to see the evidence of the fourth industrial revolution starting to cut into that front line of new job creation," Poloz says. Bearing the brunt are Canada's youth, Poloz adds, noting that the unemployment rate for the 15-to-24 cohort sits at 14.5%, or well above the 7.1% national jobless rate. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

data.title: ICE Review: Canola Turns Weaker
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Intercontinental Exchange canola futures fall back on Friday, due to pressure from a variety of sources.

That included losses in MATIF rapeseed and Malaysian palm oil, as well as Chicago soymeal. However, canola's declines were tempered by gains in Chicago soybean and soyoil. Upticks in crude oil underpinned the vegetable oils.

Today's losses pulled the November canola contract further below its 20-day moving average of C$624.15 per tonne.

Harvest pressure was beginning to increase canola. Saskatchewan reported on Thursday its canola was 42 per cent completed and ahead of Alberta's next crop report, its canola will be above last week's 28 per cent finished.

Canola exports continued to struggle with the Canadian Grain Commission reporting year-to-date volume at 627,400 tonnes versus 1.68 million the same time last year. However, domestic use of 1.61 million tonnes is slightly ahead of a year ago.

As good weather for combining has been forecast for most of the Prairies, there's speculation in the trade that canola production could reach or exceed 21 million tonnes. Earlier this month, Statistics Canada estimated the canola crops at 20.03 million tonnes.

The Canadian dollar was a pinch lower on Friday afternoon with the loonie at 71.74 U.S. cents, compared to Thursday's close of 71.80.

There were 42,205 contracts traded on Friday, compared to 42,073 on Thursday. Spreading accounted for 26,804 contracts traded.

Prices are in Canadian dollars per metric tonne:

data.title: Electronic Arts Stock Jumps on Report of Leveraged Buyout Talks — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Staff

Shares in videogame maker Electronic Arts rose 15% after The Wall Street Journal reported that the company is nearing a deal to go private. A group of investors including private-equity firm Silver Lake and Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund could unveil a deal for the publisher best known for its sports games as soon as next week. Read more of the exclusive details:

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Sinclair Will Bring Kimmel Back to Its ABC Affiliates — WSJ
data.description:
By Joseph De Avila

Sinclair is putting Jimmy Kimmel back on the air.

The TV broadcaster, which owns dozens of ABC affiliates in markets including Washington, D.C., Seattle and St. Louis, said it will end its pre-emption of "Jimmy Kimmel Live!" and air the late-night show again beginning Friday.

ABC suspended the show earlier this month after remarks Kimmel made about the killing of conservative activist and podcast host Charlie Kirk. ABC brought the show back Tuesday, but Sinclair said it still wouldn't carry it at that time.

"Our decision to preempt this program was independent of any government interaction or influence," Sinclair said Friday. "Free speech provides broadcasters with the right to exercise judgment as to the content on their local stations."

Nexstar Media Group, another major broadcaster that pulled Kimmel from its local ABC affiliates, said earlier this week that it continues to evaluate the show and is speaking with Disney, "with a focus on ensuring the program reflects and respects the diverse interests of the communities we serve." Nexstar didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

Last week, after Federal Communications Commission Chairman Brendan Carr suggested the agency might come down on broadcasters that didn't take action against Kimmel, Sinclair and Nexstar said they would pull the show from their local affiliates. ABC then suspended it for four days.

Sinclair had initially called for Kimmel to apologize to the Kirk family and make a personal donation to the family and Kirk's organization, Turning Point USA.

Sinclair said it has received feedback from viewers, advertisers and others since pulling "Jimmy Kimmel Live!" and "remains committed to serving our communities with programming that reflects their priorities, earns their trust, and promotes constructive dialogue."

Sinclair said it has had discussions with ABC, and proposed "measures to strengthen accountability, viewer feedback and community dialogue." Sinclair also proposed adding a network-wide independent ombudsman, it said.

Sinclair said it respects the right of ABC and its parent, Disney, to make such decisions.

ABC hasn't made any editorial or content concessions, people familiar with the matter said.

Write to Joseph De Avila at joseph.deavila@wsj.com

data.title: Wheat Futures Dip Amid Ample Global Supply
data.description:
Wheat futures fell below $5.20 per bushel on Friday as traders adjusted positions ahead of the quarter’s end and the upcoming US grain stocks report.

Ample global supplies continued to weigh on the market, keeping prices under pressure.

Analysts expect the USDA to report domestic wheat stocks at 2.043 billion bushels on September 1, up from 1.992 billion a year earlier, while all-wheat production is estimated at 1.925 billion bushels, largely unchanged from prior forecasts.

Strong crop outlooks, including the European Commission’s projection of a 10-year high EU soft wheat output of 132.6 million metric tons, reinforced the market’s heavy fundamentals.

Traders are also monitoring the effects of a brief suspension of Argentina’s grain export taxes, which sparked a short-term surge in sales.

In France, vessels are loading significant wheat shipments for Egypt, though export demand for the coming months remains light.

data.title: Gold Fuels Stellar Performance in Toronto Stock Market — Market Talk
data.description:
1503 ET - Canada's main stock market in Toronto has enjoyed a stellar year, with year-to-date gains now exceeding 20% after three quarters. National Bank Financial says Toronto stocks have benefited from a rush into gold, as traders deal with persistent inflation uncertainty fueled by the Trump administration's tariffs and USD depreciation. Gold stocks have surged, and NBF calculates that gold stocks account for 11% of the market capitalization of Toronto-listed equities, or the highest share since an 11.5% peak in 2012. The historical average is 4.7% based on records dating back to the mid-1970s. NBF says it expects the share of market capitalization of gold stocks to surpass the 2012 peak as gold remains attractive as an alternative asset. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1450 ET - Costco Wholesale could gain more sales growth from its extended hours for executive members, Bank of America analysts Robert Ohmes and Madeline Cech say. The retailer gained 1% in weekly U.S. sales from the extended hours during the F4Q after implementing them in June. The analysts expect that boost to continue, along with benefits from Costco's membership fee increase and planned warehouse openings in fiscal 2026. Costco paid membership and executive membership penetration is still growing, which, for Ohmes and Cech, offsets any concern about recent declines in overall membership renewal rates. Costco is off 2.7%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1428 ET - The tariff landscape for the furniture industry is in a fluid situation after President Trump promised to impose a 30% levy on furniture imports, Raymond James analyst Bobby Griffin tells WSJ in an interview. Griffin says investors were already expecting some degree of tariffs after Trump in August said furniture imports would be investigated. But it is still uncertain whether the new rates will be stacked upon existing reciprocal tariff rates with particular countries or will replace those rates. That could make a big difference with many furniture companies already dealing with high tariffs for their imports from Vietnam and China. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1337 ET - Ulta is in a better spot to face competition as industry dynamics improve while the company makes progress on its internal execution, UBS analysts say in a note. Demand seems to be improving across the beauty industry while promotions appear rational, setting up a supportive backdrop for Ulta to see a high return on investment as it invests further in its product assortment and guest experience, the analysts say. The company is also poised to gain market share as drug and department stores continue to close locations, they say. Such dynamics should further support the stock's outperformance, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1331 ET - Consolidation in the homebuilding industry has helped make it better able to keep building through downturns, according to John Burns Research & Consulting analyst Jody Kahn. Big builders with significant capital compose 61% of the market in 2024, up from 24% in 2003, a portion that will likely keep growing. These builders can better secure low-cost financing, compete for land and labor and negotiate for lower prices, making the industry less sensitive to demand cycles. "Smaller local builders often struggle during periods of low demand, while larger, capital-backed homebuilders are better positioned to keep building, capitalize on growth cycles, and potentially stabilize the new home sector," Kahn says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1319 ET - Speciality beauty and mass retailers continue to beat out drug and department stores in the beauty category, according to UBS in a note. The analysts say they found a sharp discrepancy in sales performance between players like Ulta, Sephora, Walmart and Amazon versus the likes of CVS, RiteAid, Walgreens, Macy's, Kohl's and Nordstrom. Those drug and department stores also continue to close stores, shutting down several hundred in the first half of the year, they say. That should allow room for further market share gains from differentiated players like Ulta going forward, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1309 ET - Okta's security product for AI agents likely won't add any material contributions for the company's growth until fiscal 2028 at the earliest, D.A. Davidson analysts Rudy Kessinger and Andres Miranda Lopez say in a note after attending the company's annual user event. The product will be available in fiscal 2027 and aims to discover risky agents and centrally control agent access, among other functions, the analysts say. Several customers said they are interested in learning more about the offering, but the vast majority are not planning any significant AI agent rollouts anytime soon, the analysts say. Okta management indicates the product will not be a big growth opportunity in the near term, but should be significant in the long term, the analysts say.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1259 ET - KB Home may face obstacles in its path back to build-to-order homes, say Zelman & Associates analysts. Right now, there is an abundance of spec inventory on the market and KB Home's competitors are offering aggressive incentives to offload it. That may make it difficult for the company to compete using its build-to-order offerings, especially as it has adopted the strategy of cutting price rather than offering incentives. "It remains to be seen if KBH is able to differentiate itself as a build-to-order builder focusing on transparent pricing rather than an incentive-heavy pricing approach," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1256 ET - Costco Wholesale delivered solid results in the F4Q, but it's increasingly difficult for the company to meet expectations as it's considered one of the best retailers in the world, DA Davidson analysts Michael Baker and Keegan Cox say. Investors already knew what to expect from sales because of Costco's monthly reporting, but incremental numbers were mixed, Baker and Cox say. Membership fee income beat expectations and gross margins were up as expected, but EBIT dollars were slightly below consensus estimates and sales growth slowed, they say. Costco is off 2.3%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1253 ET - KB Home said it wants to get back to its historic levels of building 70% of its homes to order rather than on spec. That's a worthwhile long-term goal, but its success will depend on strength of demand during the spring selling season, say Zelman & Associates analysts. Historically, the company has built a competitive advantage and higher margins through build-to-order homes. But right now, the company's backlog is down 22% year-over-year, which means it will need significant order growth in the first half of next year. KB Home also has less spec inventory to use as a buffer if demand for its build-to-order homes doesn't pick up.(nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1241 ET - Costco Wholesale's valuation doesn't match its slowing key performance indicators, Roth analyst Bill Kirk says. The retailer's same-store sales growth has been slowing down since it hiked membership fees, and U.S. growth hit a six-quarter low in 4Q. Membership renewal rates are also weakening. Kirk thinks Costco's performance will start to look more similar to conventional food retailers and will struggle to keep gaining as much market share as it has in recent years, as it gets more competition from Walmart and BJ's. "In our view, the valuation discrepancy between Costco and conventional peers is too wide," Kirk says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1233 ET - Okta's customers appear increasingly interested in its identity governance offerings, D.A. Davidson analysts Rudy Kessinger and Andres Miranda Lopez say in a note after meeting with customers and channel partners at the company's annual user event. Customers shared modestly better feedback on Okta's identity governance and privileged access management products than the analysts observed in recent field work, they say. Okta's identity governance offerings are resonating particularly well with small- and mid-sized businesses, though larger enterprises are still predominantly using competitor SailPoint, they say.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.description:

In the Dallas Fed's 3Q survey of energy companies, 42% of exploration and production executives said they have slightly delayed investment decisions because of oil price uncertainty or cost of production, and 36% said they have significantly delayed decisions. On regulatory changes made since January, 57% said they had reduced break-even costs for new wells by less than $1 a barrel, and 25% estimated reductions between $1 and $1.99 a barrel. Rigs directed at natural gas this week slipped by one to 117, or 18 more than a year ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Ukraine says Russia drone incursion part of pressure plan against West
data.description:
Key points:
  • NATO jets downed Russian drones in Polish airspace
  • Russia denies targeting Poland, Trump cites possible mistake
  • Kremlin calls talk of shooting down Russian planes reckless

By Tom Balmforth

Russia deliberately flew drones into the airspace of NATO member Poland this month to test the alliance's response, stoke fatigue in member states with its war on Ukraine and step up pressure on the West, the Ukrainian military spy agency said on Friday.

It sent the written assessment to Reuters after NATO jets downed Russian drones that entered Polish airspace on September 9-10. This week unidentified drones shut down air traffic in parts of Denmark.

The assessment only addressed the incident in Poland.

"Such hybrid operations likely aim to increase pressure on Ukraine's Western partners, potentially leading to: Reduced support for Ukraine, especially military aid," the agency wrote.

Russia denies it planned to target anything in Poland with drones as it was attacking neighbouring Ukraine. U.S. President Donald Trump said in the immediate aftermath that it was possible the drones had ended up there by mistake.

Ukraine uses jamming and electronic systems to try to suppress and divert incoming Russian drone and missile strikes, but the Ukrainian spy agency said it had ruled that out as the cause.

"UAVs (drones) entered Poland not only from Ukraine but also from Belarus. Some UAVs penetrated up to 100 km (60 miles) into Polish territory, far beyond the range of Ukrainian electronic warfare systems," it said.

It also pointed to the large number of drones - around 20, it estimated - involved in the incident. The models included the Gerbera decoy drone, the use of which indicated that Russia had not intended to hit anything, the Ukrainian agency said.

'PART OF BROADER PLAN'

Tensions have mounted on NATO's eastern flank with Russia in recent weeks. Apart from the Polish incursion, Estonia accused Russia of sending three fighter jets into its airspace last week, while Romania has come close to shooting down a drone.

Earlier on Friday, the Kremlin said that talk of shooting down Russian military planes over Europe was reckless, aggressive and marked a serious escalation of tension near Russia's border.

Without addressing the more recent incidents, the Ukrainian agency said: "This incident (in Poland) is part of Russia's broader plan to expand aggression into countries bordering Ukraine".

"It serves as a warning to NATO and a test of their response to attacks on member states. It is also intended to intimidate the Polish population."

It assessed there had been 10 violations of Moldovan airspace by Russian drones this year, and that 14 drones had crashed on Romanian territory during attacks on nearby port infrastructure in Ukraine since 2023.

"Violations of airspace in countries bordering Ukraine have become systematic during Russian combined air attacks on Ukrainian civilian and critical infrastructure," it said.

data.title: Big Pharma Unfazed by Trump Tariffs, But Small Biotechs Face Vulnerability — WSJ
data.description:
By Joseph Walker and Peter Loftus

President Trump's plan for new tariffs on pharmaceuticals landed like a bomb on social media late Thursday, but many of the world's richest drugmakers say they expect to be unscathed by the new levies.

Smaller biotechs are a different story.

Trump announced on Truth Social that brand-name drugs would be tariffed at 100% starting next month, but provided a big escape hatch: "unless a company IS BUILDING their pharmaceutical manufacturing plant in America, " the president wrote.

AstraZeneca said on Friday it expects to be exempt from the new tariffs because it has significant manufacturing operations in the U.S., as well as new facilities under construction. Novartis said it should be unaffected because it has ongoing construction projects and new ones it will announce before the end of the year.

Several other companies including Gilead Sciences, Regeneron and Roche stopped short of saying that they would be exempt, but pointed to their construction of new manufacturing sites in statements sent to The Wall Street Journal.

"Right now, cranes are rising at Novo Nordisk's U.S. manufacturing site in North Carolina, where we are halfway through a [$]4.1 billion expansion," Chief Executive Mike Doustdar said through a spokeswoman.

Big drugmakers have been bracing for tariffs since the start of the Trump administration, and have sought to get ahead of new levies by pledging to invest more than $350 billion on manufacturing, research and development and other functions in the U.S., the Journal has reported. Hedging their bets even further, companies like Eli Lilly have rushed to import large quantities of key products and stockpile inventory before any new tariffs come into place.

However, smaller companies that produce only one or two drugs may be hit harder because they tend to rely on overseas contract manufacturers.

"The immediacy of punitive, 100% tariffs on innovative medicines for any company without 'shovels in the ground' would devastate our nation's small and midsize biotechnology companies," said John F. Crowley, chief executive of the Biotechnology Innovation Organization, an industry trade group. "Most of these companies do not have access to the significant capital needed to immediately establish manufacturing in the U.S."

The timing of Trump's announcement and the scarcity of details accompanying it took many industry executives and analysts by surprise. The 63-word post created a number of unanswered questions on who would qualify for exemptions.

Will each product a company makes need to be manufactured in the U.S., or is a single new manufacturing construction project enough? Will the tariffs be applied on top of U.S. trade agreements with the European Union and Japan that cap tariffs at 15%?

"Like everyone else, we're scrambling to figure it out," said a senior pharmaceutical executive. He noted that many of the industry's new manufacturing projects are intended to produce new pipeline products rather than existing drugs that are made overseas.

If the EU and Japan are excluded, the new tariffs would most likely affect drugs made in the U.K., Singapore and Switzerland, countries that have significant pharmaceutical manufacturing.

GSK, Novartis and Roche all have manufacturing in either the U.K. or Switzerland, or both, and would be most heavily impacted, according to Morningstar analyst Jay Lee.

However, each of those companies also has announced new manufacturing projects in the U.S., which would seem to exempt them, going by the limited details released by Trump on Thursday night.

The plan to exempt companies that are actively building new manufacturing in the U.S. raises questions about enforcement of that provision. Most big drugmakers have announced plans to build in news releases and news conferences, but if the government requires more substantial documentation that construction is actually under way, it could expose more branded drugs to the tariffs as of Oct. 1, Lee said.

There is also no real way for the administration to verify which products will be made at new manufacturing sites, said Evan Seigerman, a BMO Capital Markets biopharma analyst. New plants take years to build and likely won't be completed until after Trump's second term ends, he said.

The president appears to be "shooting from the hip," said Seigerman. "It shows the challenges of policy without real substance, because the sector of pharmaceuticals is complicated, and if you want to apply tariffs, you need to be specific."

Bernstein analysts said in a research note Friday that the new tariffs are "a pretty good outcome" for the pharmaceutical industry, assuming the EU is capped at 15%. "Trump also offers up a 'get out of jail free card' for companies that bend the knee and demonstrate plans to build more manufacturing plants in the U.S.," the analysts wrote.

Write to Joseph Walker at joseph.walker@wsj.com and Peter Loftus at Peter.Loftus@wsj.com

data.title: The Wealthy Are Paying Their Adult Kids' Bills. Some of Them Are Starting to Worry. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By David Wignall

The great majority of wealthy parents are giving their adult children financial support, according to a new report released by Ameriprise Financial. In a survey of 554 parents with an average of roughly $750,000 in investible assets, all of whom had adult children, 76% say they are footing the bill for their children's big-ticket items, like down payments on a home or graduate degrees. Nearly two-thirds (63%) are covering ongoing costs like phone bills and other living costs. And 98% are willing to let adult children live with them after they turn 21.

The results demonstrate the prominent role the "Bank of Mom and Dad" now plays in the everyday life of America's mass affluent class. But that bank may not be making a profit. While 65% of parents believe they have enough money to live comfortably in retirement, 36% worry that supporting adult children could hamper their plans, according to the survey.

Deana Healy, Ameriprise's vice president of financial planning and advice, says poor communication is part of the problem. If parents don't set clear expectations about which expenses are off-limits and when support might end, they can end up on the hook later in life. To make matters worse, their children might not take the appropriate steps to establish financial independence.

Rising costs. Several factors may be contributing to the growing role affluent parents play in their children's financial lives.

First, the cost of living has been rising rapidly in real terms, especially in major U.S. cities. As rents soar and the early-career job market looks increasingly shaky, children are turning to their parents for support.

"There are certainly headwinds in today's environment that perhaps make this more challenging for a young adult entering young adulthood," says Healy.

Young people are also achieving major life milestones — such as full-time work, marriage, homeownership, and parenthood — later in life. For example, in 2021, 39% of 21-year-olds were working full time, compared with 64% in 1980, according to Pew Research. About two-thirds (68%) of 25-year-olds were living outside their parents' home, compared with 84% in 1980. Much of the slowdown is due to rising college participation, as college enrollees delay their income-earning years.

But the wealth of older Americans also explains some of the trend. Because today's baby boomers are the richest generation in history, they have more resources to dedicate to their children. Many are therefore choosing to give their children a leg up — whether that means cosigning for a credit card, paying for medical school, or buying a house.

Corrections & Amplifications: Ameriprise surveyed 554 respondents with an average of roughly $750,000 in investible assets. A previous version of the article stated that the survey received 545 responses from parents with roughly $500,000 in assets.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: XRP Fast Price Lane Revealed as Ripple Co-Founder Makes Bold XRP Thesis Against SWIFT
data.description: Top market analyst Ali Martinez has suggested that XRP could be on the verge of a breakout, provided it holds above the key $2.71 support.
